topics.title: Circular Convolution and Parseval's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECL507 - Digital Signal Processing Lab
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Circular Convolution and Parseval's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Circular Convolution - Formal Academic Definition

**Circular Convolution** is a discrete-time convolution operation defined between two finite-duration sequences that are inherently treated as **periodic with period $N$**. The circular convolution of two sequences $x[n]$ and $h[n]$, each of length $N$, yields an output sequence $y[n]$ that is also periodic with period $N$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Circular convolution is the operation performed by the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) domain multiplication: if $Y[k] = X[k] \cdot H[k]$, then $y[n]$ is the circular convolution of $x[n]$ and $h[n]$, denoted $y[n] = x[n] \circledast_N h[n]$.

The mathematical definition:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \cdot h[(n-k) \mod N], \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

> [!NOTE]
> The modulo-$N$ operation on the index is the defining feature that distinguishes **circular convolution** from **linear convolution**. It causes the sequence to "wrap around" the index circle, which is why it is called *circular*.

## 1.2 Parseval's Theorem - Formal Academic Definition

**Parseval's Theorem for DFT** (also called the **Energy Conservation Theorem**) states that the total energy of a finite-duration sequence computed in the time domain is equal to the total energy of its DFT spectrum divided by $N$, computed in the frequency domain.

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This theorem provides a powerful **verification tool** in KTU lab examinations: a student can cross-check whether the computed DFT is correct by confirming whether time-domain energy equals frequency-domain energy (divided by $N$).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Circular Convolution Analogy — The Clock Face:**
Imagine $x[n]$ and $h[n]$ as the numbers **1 to 12** printed on a clock face. To compute circular convolution at $n=3$, you rotate the second clock (containing $h$) by 3 positions and then multiply overlapping numbers, summing the products. Because the clock face is *circular*, the numbers wrap around — there is no "edge" like in linear convolution. Each output sample is the dot product of one stationary sequence and one *rotated* sequence.

**Parseval's Theorem Analogy — Energy Banking:**
Think of a sequence's total energy as a sum of money in a bank. You can deposit this money as **time-domain cash** (squared samples) or convert it to **frequency-domain foreign currency** (squared DFT magnitudes, scaled by $1/N$). The total wealth remains the same regardless of the currency used. This is the *conservation of energy* principle in signal processing.

## 1.4 Key Distinguishing Features

| Feature | Linear Convolution | Circular Convolution |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Periodicity | Aperiodic output | Periodic output (period $N$) |
| Output length | $N_1 + N_2 - 1$ | $\max(N_1, N_2)$ |
| Operation | Standard summation | Uses modulo-$N$ on index |
| Equivalent in DFT | Requires zero-padding | Direct DFT multiplication |
| Use case | Real-world filtering | Efficient block processing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (bolded):**
> - **Length $N$** = DFT block size, must be $\geq \max(N_1, N_2)$
> - **Normalization factor** = $1/N$ for Parseval's theorem
> - **DFT periodicity** = **$N$** samples per fundamental period
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of Circular Convolution

The circular convolution of two $N$-point sequences $x[n]$ and $h[n]$ produces an $N$-point output $y[n]$:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \cdot h[((n-k))_N]$$

where $((m))_N$ denotes $m \mod N$ (the remainder when $m$ is divided by $N$).

### Why the "Circular" Behavior?

When the index $(n-k)$ becomes negative, modulo arithmetic wraps it back to the end of the sequence. This means:
- $h[-1] \rightarrow h[N-1]$
- $h[-2] \rightarrow h[N-2]$
- $h[N] \rightarrow h[0]$
- $h[N+1] \rightarrow h[1]$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Classical Methods (KTU lab exam favourites):**
> 1. **Concentric Circle Method** (graphical, intuitive)
> 2. **Matrix Method** (systematic, tabular)
> 3. **DFT–IDFT Method** (computationally elegant: $y[n] = \text{IDFT}\{X[k] \cdot H[k]\}$)

## 2.2 Relationship between Linear and Circular Convolution

If $x[n]$ has length $N_1$ and $h[n]$ has length $N_2$, and we perform circular convolution on $N$-point sequences (where $N \geq \max(N_1, N_2)$), the result equals the linear convolution **only when $N \geq N_1 + N_2 - 1$**.

When $N < N_1 + N_2 - 1$, the **time-aliased** linear convolution produces the circular convolution. This principle is called **aliasing in time domain** and is the foundation of the **Overlap-Add** and **Overlap-Save** methods.

## 2.3 Parseval's Theorem — Energy Conservation

For a sequence $x[n]$ of length $N$ with DFT $X[k]$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$$

### Physical Interpretation

- **LHS (Time Domain):** Total signal energy computed directly from samples.
- **RHS (Frequency Domain):** Total signal energy computed from spectral magnitudes, scaled by $1/N$.

The factor $1/N$ arises because the DFT computation distributes the energy across $N$ frequency bins.

## 2.4 Generalized Parseval's Theorem (for inner products)

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot y^*[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot Y^*[k]$$

This is a more general form useful for cross-correlation analysis.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Domain** |
|------------|----------------|------------|
| $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \cdot h[((n-k))_N]$ | Circular convolution definition | Time |
| $y[n] = x[n] \circledast_N h[n] = \text{IDFT}\{X[k] \cdot H[k]\}$ | DFT-domain equivalent | Mixed |
| $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$ | DFT of $x[n]$ | Frequency |
| $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ | IDFT of $X[k]$ | Time |
| $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | Parseval's theorem (energy) | Energy |
| $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]y^*[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]Y^*[k]$ | Generalized Parseval | Inner product |
| $N \geq N_1 + N_2 - 1$ | Condition for circular = linear | Length |
| $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ | Twiddle factor (DFT kernel) | Constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Circular Convolution:** Used in **OFDM systems** (4G/5G wireless), **block convolution filtering** in real-time DSP chips, and **fast polynomial multiplication** (FFT-based).
> - **Parseval's Theorem:** Used in **spectrum analyzers** for power verification, **audio codec design** (MP3/AAC) for energy-based bit allocation, and **noise power estimation** in communication receivers.

## 2.6 The "Why" Behind Each Step

1. **Why modulo $N$?** Because the DFT inherently assumes periodicity of period $N$. Multiplication in DFT domain equals circular (not linear) convolution in time domain.
2. **Why factor $1/N$ in Parseval's?** Because the IDFT includes a $1/N$ scaling; this scaling must be reflected in the energy calculation to maintain equality.
3. **Why pad with zeros for linear convolution?** To prevent the time-domain aliasing of the linear convolution sum, ensuring circular convolution = linear convolution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Circular Convolution via DFT Property

### Statement
The DFT-domain multiplication theorem states:

$$\text{DFT}\{x[n] \circledast_N h[n]\} = X[k] \cdot H[k]$$

### Derivation

Starting from the definition of circular convolution:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \cdot h[((n-k))_N]$$

Take the DFT of $y[n]$:

$$Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} y[n] \cdot W_N^{kn}, \quad \text{where } W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$$

Substitute the definition of $y[n]$:

$$Y[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot h[((n-m))_N] \right] W_N^{kn}$$

Swap the order of summation:

$$Y[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[((n-m))_N] W_N^{kn} \right]$$

Let $r = (n-m) \mod N$. As $n$ ranges over $0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, $r$ also ranges over $0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ (due to periodicity). Then $n = r + m$ (mod $N$).

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h[((n-m))_N] W_N^{kn} = \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} h[r] \cdot W_N^{k(r+m)}$$

$$= W_N^{km} \sum_{r=0}^{N-1} h[r] \cdot W_N^{kr} = W_N^{km} \cdot H[k]$$

Therefore:

$$Y[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \cdot W_N^{km} \cdot H[k] = H[k] \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] W_N^{km}$$

$$Y[k] = X[k] \cdot H[k] \quad \blacksquare$$

### Conclusion
Multiplication in the DFT domain corresponds to circular convolution in the time domain, **proving** the central relationship used in KTU experiments.

---

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE DERIVATION: Parseval's Theorem

### Statement
For an $N$-point sequence $x[n]$ with DFT $X[k]$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$$

### Derivation

Start with the LHS — time-domain energy:

$$E_{\text{time}} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot x^*[n]$$

Substitute the IDFT expression for $x[n]$:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \cdot W_N^{-kn}$$

Replace $x^*[n]$ using the conjugate property of IDFT:

$$x^*[n] = \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn} \right]^* = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X^*[k] W_N^{kn}$$

Now compute the energy:

$$E_{\text{time}} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn} \right] \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} X^*[m] W_N^{mn} \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} X[k] X^*[m] \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} W_N^{n(m-k)}$$

Evaluate the inner sum over $n$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} W_N^{n(m-k)} = \begin{cases} N & \text{if } m = k \\ 0 & \text{if } m \neq k \end{cases}$$

This is the **orthogonality property** of complex exponentials.

Therefore, the sum collapses to $m = k$ only:

$$E_{\text{time}} = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] X^*[k] \cdot N = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{time}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE: Circular Convolution by Matrix Method

**Problem:** Compute the 4-point circular convolution of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $h[n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}$.

### Step 1: Construct the circulant matrix of $h[n]$

The circulant matrix has $h$ in the first row, right-shifted cyclically in subsequent rows:

$$H_{\text{circ}} = \begin{bmatrix} h[0] & h[1] & h[2] & h[3] \\ h[3] & h[0] & h[1] & h[2] \\ h[2] & h[3] & h[0] & h[1] \\ h[1] & h[2] & h[3] & h[0] \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Multiply by column vector $x$

$$y = H_{\text{circ}} \cdot x = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Compute each output

$$y[0] = 4(1) + 3(2) + 2(3) + 1(4) = 4 + 6 + 6 + 4 = 20$$

$$y[1] = 1(1) + 4(2) + 3(3) + 2(4) = 1 + 8 + 9 + 8 = 26$$

$$y[2] = 2(1) + 1(2) + 4(3) + 3(4) = 2 + 2 + 12 + 12 = 28$$

$$y[3] = 3(1) + 2(2) + 1(3) + 4(4) = 3 + 4 + 3 + 16 = 26$$

### Final Answer

$$y[n] = \{20, 26, 28, 26\}$$

### Step 4: Verification by Linear Convolution (for comparison)

The linear convolution is:

$$x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, \quad h[n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}$$

$$y_{\text{lin}}[0] = 1 \cdot 4 = 4$$
$$y_{\text{lin}}[1] = 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 = 11$$
$$y_{\text{lin}}[2] = 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 4 = 20$$
$$y_{\text{lin}}[3] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 3 + 4 \cdot 4 = 30$$
$$y_{\text{lin}}[4] = 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 2 + 4 \cdot 3 = 20$$
$$y_{\text{lin}}[5] = 3 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 2 = 11$$
$$y_{\text{lin}}[6] = 4 \cdot 1 = 4$$

Linear result: $\{4, 11, 20, 30, 20, 11, 4\}$ (length 7)

The circular result $\{20, 26, 28, 26\}$ is **NOT** equal to linear convolution because the length condition $N \geq N_1 + N_2 - 1 = 7$ is not satisfied.

---

## 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE: Parseval's Theorem Verification

**Problem:** Verify Parseval's theorem for $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (4-point DFT).

### Step 1: Compute Time-Domain Energy

$$E_{\text{time}} = \sum_{n=0}^{3} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 = 30$$

### Step 2: Compute 4-point DFT

Using $W_4 = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$:

$$X[0] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

$$X[1] = 1 + 2(-j) + 3(-1) + 4(j) = 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2 + 2j$$

$$X[2] = 1 + 2(-1) + 3(1) + 4(-1) = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2$$

$$X[3] = 1 + 2(j) + 3(-1) + 4(-j) = 1 + 2j - 3 - 4j = -2 - 2j$$

### Step 3: Compute Frequency-Domain Energy

$$\vert X[0] \vert^2 = 100$$
$$\vert X[1] \vert^2 = (-2)^2 + (2)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$
$$\vert X[2] \vert^2 = (-2)^2 = 4$$
$$\vert X[3] \vert^2 = (-2)^2 + (-2)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$

$$\sum_{k=0}^{3} \vert X[k] \vert^2 = 100 + 8 + 4 + 8 = 120$$

### Step 4: Verify

$$\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{3} \vert X[k] \vert^2 = \frac{120}{4} = 30 = E_{\text{time}} \quad \checkmark$$

**Parseval's theorem is verified.**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define circular convolution. How does it differ from linear convolution?**

**Model Answer:**

Circular convolution is a discrete convolution operation defined between two periodic sequences of period $N$, given by:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \cdot h[((n-k))_N]$$

It differs from linear convolution in three key ways:
1. The output is **periodic** with period $N$, whereas linear convolution is **aperiodic**.
2. The **modulo-$N$** operation on the index $(n-k)$ causes wrap-around, while linear convolution uses standard index arithmetic.
3. Linear convolution produces output of length $N_1 + N_2 - 1$, but circular convolution produces output of length $N = \max(N_1, N_2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two are **equal** only when $N \geq N_1 + N_2 - 1$ (i.e., when zero-padding is sufficient to prevent time-domain aliasing).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Parseval's theorem for DFT.**

**Model Answer:**

Parseval's theorem states that the total energy of a finite-duration sequence $x[n]$ is conserved between the time domain and the frequency domain:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$$

where $X[k]$ is the $N$-point DFT of $x[n]$.

**Significance:** It guarantees that the energy computed directly from time-domain samples equals the energy computed from frequency-domain magnitudes (scaled by $1/N$). This is used in DSP applications to:
- Verify the correctness of DFT computation
- Calculate signal power from spectrum analyzer readings
- Implement energy-based detection in communication systems

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Compute the 4-point circular convolution of the sequences $x[n] = \{1, 2, 1, 2\}$ and $h[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ using (i) the matrix method and (ii) the concentric circle method. Verify your result using the DFT-IDFT method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-point sequence $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ has DFT $X[k] = \{4, 0, 0, 0\}$. Verify Parseval's theorem for this sequence. State the engineering significance of the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Matrix Method:**

Construct the circulant matrix of $h[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$:

$$H_{\text{circ}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & 4 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply by $x = [1, 2, 1, 2]^T$:

$$y[0] = 1(1) + 2(2) + 3(1) + 4(2) = 1 + 4 + 3 + 8 = 16$$

$$y[1] = 4(1) + 1(2) + 2(1) + 3(2) = 4 + 2 + 2 + 6 = 14$$

$$y[2] = 3(1) + 4(2) + 1(1) + 2(2) = 3 + 8 + 1 + 4 = 16$$

$$y[3] = 2(1) + 3(2) + 4(1) + 1(2) = 2 + 6 + 4 + 2 = 14$$

$$y[n] = \{16, 14, 16, 14\}$$

**Step 2 — Concentric Circle Method:**

Place $x[n] = \{1, 2, 1, 2\}$ on the outer circle and $h[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ on the inner circle (both at position $n=0$).

- **For $n=0$:** No rotation. Multiply and sum: $1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 = 1 + 4 + 3 + 8 = 16$ ✓
- **For $n=1$:** Rotate inner circle clockwise by 1. New arrangement: $\{2, 3, 4, 1\}$. Multiply: $1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 1 = 2 + 6 + 4 + 2 = 14$ ✓
- **For $n=2$:** Rotate inner by 2. New arrangement: $\{3, 4, 1, 2\}$. Multiply: $1 \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 3 + 8 + 1 + 4 = 16$ ✓
- **For $n=3$:** Rotate inner by 3. New arrangement: $\{4, 1, 2, 3\}$. Multiply: $1 \cdot 4 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 = 4 + 2 + 2 + 6 = 14$ ✓

**Step 3 — Verification by DFT-IDFT Method:**

Using $W_4 = e^{-j\pi/2} = -j$:

$X[0] = 1+2+1+2 = 6$, $X[1] = 1-2j-1+2j = 0$, $X[2] = 1-2+1-2 = -2$, $X[3] = 1+2j-1-2j = 0$

$H[0] = 1+2+3+4 = 10$, $H[1] = 1-2j-3+4j = -2+2j$, $H[2] = 1-2+3-4 = -2$, $H[3] = 1+2j-3-4j = -2-2j$

$Y[k] = X[k] \cdot H[k]$:
- $Y[0] = 6 \cdot 10 = 60$
- $Y[1] = 0 \cdot (-2+2j) = 0$
- $Y[2] = -2 \cdot -2 = 4$
- $Y[3] = 0 \cdot (-2-2j) = 0$

Now compute IDFT of $Y[k]$:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} Y[k] W_4^{-kn} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{k=0}^{3} Y[k] e^{j\pi kn/2}$$

For $n=0$: $y[0] = \frac{1}{4}(60 + 0 + 4 + 0) = 16$ ✓

For $n=1$: $y[1] = \frac{1}{4}(60 + 0 + 4 \cdot e^{j\pi} + 0) = \frac{1}{4}(60 - 4) = 14$ ✓

For $n=2$: $y[2] = \frac{1}{4}(60 + 0 + 4 \cdot e^{j2\pi} + 0) = \frac{1}{4}(60 + 4) = 16$ ✓

For $n=3$: $y[3] = \frac{1}{4}(60 + 0 + 4 \cdot e^{j3\pi} + 0) = \frac{1}{4}(60 - 4) = 14$ ✓

**Result verified:** $y[n] = \{16, 14, 16, 14\}$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Time-Domain Energy:**

$$E_{\text{time}} = \sum_{n=0}^{3} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Frequency-Domain Energy:**

$$\sum_{k=0}^{3} \vert X[k] \vert^2 = \vert 4 \vert^2 + \vert 0 \vert^2 + \vert 0 \vert^2 + \vert 0 \vert^2 = 16$$

$$\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{3} \vert X[k] \vert^2 = \frac{16}{4} = 4$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**

$$E_{\text{time}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{3} \vert X[k] \vert^2 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$

**Parseval's theorem is verified.**

**Step 4 — Engineering Significance:**

1. The DC component $X[0] = 4$ represents the sum of all samples (the average value multiplied by $N$).
2. The absence of higher-frequency components ($X[1] = X[2] = X[3] = 0$) confirms $x[n]$ is a **constant sequence**, which has only a DC spectral component.
3. This result is significant because in **power system analysis** and **DC signal measurement**, a constant input has all its energy concentrated at zero frequency.
4. Parseval's theorem confirms the energy is correctly accounted for in both domains — a fundamental property used in **spectrum analyzers** and **signal integrity verification**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the three methods of computing circular convolution with suitable diagrams. Compute the 5-point circular convolution of $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and $h[n] = \{5, 4, 3, 2, 1\}$ using the matrix method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ (8-point sequence), the 8-point DFT is computed. Using Parseval's theorem, find the energy of the signal in both domains and verify the relationship. Given that $X[0] = 36$, $X[4] = -4$, and all other $X[k] = 0$, show the energy conservation numerically. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Three Methods of Circular Convolution:**

**Method 1: Concentric Circle Method**
- Draw two concentric circles of $N$ divisions.
- Plot $x[n]$ values on the outer circle (clockwise).
- Plot $h[n]$ values on the inner circle.
- For each output $y[n]$, rotate the inner circle by $n$ positions and compute the sum of products of overlapping values.

**Method 2: Matrix Method**
- Form the $N \times N$ circulant matrix using $h[n]$ in the first row, with each subsequent row being a cyclic right-shift of the previous.
- Multiply this matrix by the column vector $x[n]$ to obtain $y[n]$.

**Method 3: DFT-IDFT Method**
- Compute $X[k] = \text{DFT}\{x[n]\}$ and $H[k] = \text{DFT}\{h[n]\}$.
- Multiply pointwise: $Y[k] = X[k] \cdot H[k]$.
- Compute inverse: $y[n] = \text{IDFT}\{Y[k]\}$.

**Step 2 — Matrix Method Computation for 5-point case:**

Circulant matrix of $h[n] = \{5, 4, 3, 2, 1\}$:

$$H_{\text{circ}} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 4 & 3 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 5 & 4 & 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 5 & 4 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 1 & 5 & 4 \\ 4 & 3 & 2 & 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Multiply by $x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]^T$:

$$y[0] = 5(1) + 4(2) + 3(3) + 2(4) + 1(5) = 5 + 8 + 9 + 8 + 5 = 35$$

$$y[1] = 1(1) + 5(2) + 4(3) + 3(4) + 2(5) = 1 + 10 + 12 + 12 + 10 = 45$$

$$y[2] = 2(1) + 1(2) + 5(3) + 4(4) + 3(5) = 2 + 2 + 15 + 16 + 15 = 50$$

$$y[3] = 3(1) + 2(2) + 1(3) + 5(4) + 4(5) = 3 + 4 + 3 + 20 + 20 = 50$$

$$y[4] = 4(1) + 3(2) + 2(3) + 1(4) + 5(5) = 4 + 6 + 6 + 4 + 25 = 45$$

**Result:** $y[n] = \{35, 45, 50, 50, 45\}$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Time-Domain Energy:**

$$E_{\text{time}} = \sum_{n=0}^{7} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 + 25 + 36 + 49 + 64 = 204$$

**Step 2 — Frequency-Domain Energy:**

Given $X[0] = 36$, $X[4] = -4$, all other $X[k] = 0$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{7} \vert X[k] \vert^2 = \vert 36 \vert^2 + \vert -4 \vert^2 = 1296 + 16 = 1312$$

**Step 3 — Apply Parseval's Theorem:**

$$\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{7} \vert X[k] \vert^2 = \frac{1312}{8} = 164$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

- Time-domain energy: $E_{\text{time}} = 204$
- Frequency-domain energy: $\frac{1}{N} \sum \vert X[k] \vert^2 = 164$

**Note:** The values are **not equal** in this case. This indicates that the given $X[k]$ values (only $X[0]$ and $X[4]$ non-zero) do **not** represent the true DFT of $x[n] = \{1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When verifying Parseval's theorem, if the energies do not match, it signals an **error in the DFT computation** or in the given $X[k]$ values. The true DFT of an 8-point linear ramp $x[n] = n+1$ has all 8 components non-zero. This problem tests whether the student can detect inconsistencies — a critical skill in DSP debugging.

**Correction Check:** If all $X[k]$ were correctly computed, Parseval's would yield $E_{\text{time}} = 204 = \frac{1}{8} \sum \vert X[k] \vert^2$, requiring $\sum \vert X[k] \vert^2 = 1632$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the modulo operation:** Students often compute linear convolution by mistake. Always check whether indices are wrapped using `((n-k))_N`.
> 2. **Missing the $1/N$ factor in Parseval's theorem:** The frequency-domain energy must be divided by $N$ before comparison with time-domain energy. A very common error!
> 3. **Wrong circulant matrix construction:** The second row is a cyclic right-shift of the first, NOT a left-shift. The direction must be consistent with the modulo convention used in the formula.
> 4. **Indexing errors in concentric circle method:** When rotating by $n$ positions, ensure clockwise rotation matches the sign convention in your formula. Mixing clockwise and counter-clockwise rotations is a frequent error.
> 5. **Not zero-padding for linear convolution:** When asked to compute linear convolution using circular convolution, students forget to pad to length $N_1 + N_2 - 1$.
> 6. **Arithmetic mistakes in DFT computation:** The twiddle factor $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$ is complex; mixing up real and imaginary parts leads to wrong answers.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Circular Convolution Formula:** $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[k] \cdot h[((n-k))_N]$
- **DFT Multiplication Theorem:** $\text{DFT}\{x[n] \circledast_N h[n]\} = X[k] \cdot H[k]$
- **Inverse DFT Formula:** $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] W_N^{-kn}$
- **Parseval's Theorem (Energy Conservation):** $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \vert X[k] \vert^2$
- **Generalized Parseval:** $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]y^*[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]Y^*[k]$
- **Linear = Circular condition:** $N \geq N_1 + N_2 - 1$ (zero-padding to this length)
- **Three methods of circular convolution:** Concentric circle, Matrix (circulant), DFT-IDFT
- **Circulant matrix:** Each row is a cyclic right-shift of the previous row of $h$
- **Twiddle factor:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$
- **$1/N$ factor:** Always present in IDFT and Parseval's RHS — **never forget it**
- **Time-aliased samples** are the cause of circular ≠ linear convolution
- **Applications:** OFDM (4G/5G), spectrum analysis, block filtering, audio codecs
- **Engineering significance:** Parseval's theorem is the foundation of energy-based signal verification, spectrum analyzer calibration, and noise power estimation
- **Common DFT pair to remember:** $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\} \leftrightarrow X[k] = \{4, 0, 0, 0\}$ — useful for quick verification
- **Linear ramp DFT pattern:** For $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (4-point), $X[k] = \{10, -2+2j, -2, -2-2j\}$
- **Lab exam tip:** Always cross-verify circular convolution results using at least two of the three methods
- **Index convention:** When implementing in MATLAB/Python, indices run from 1 to $N$, but the formula uses 0 to $N-1$ — adjust accordingly
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of DSP Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECL507 - PBECL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of DSP Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Digital Signal Processor (DSP) Hardware** refers to specialized microprocessor architectures designed and optimized to perform mathematical operations on discrete-time signals at high speed, in real-time. Unlike general-purpose microprocessors (GPPs) such as the Intel x86 or ARM Cortex-A series, DSP hardware employs specialized architectural features — Harvard memory organization, hardware Multiplier-Accumulator (MAC) units, barrel shifters, dedicated addressing modes, and on-chip peripherals (timers, MCBSP/McASP serial ports, DMA controllers, EMIF external memory interfaces) — all of which accelerate the inner loops of signal processing algorithms such as Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filters, Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filters, Fast Fourier Transforms (FFT), and convolution.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBECL507)** lab context, *Familiarization of DSP Hardware* is the foundational experiment in which a student physically identifies, examines, and understands the functional blocks of a DSP development board (commonly the **TMS320C6748 / TMS320C6713 / TMS320C5505** or the **ADSP-2181/ADSP-BF533** kits) and the associated **Code Composer Studio (CCS)** or **VisualDSP++** Integrated Development Environment (IDE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The experiment aims to make the student proficient in identifying the CPU core, memory architecture (Program/Data/Cache), on-chip peripherals, external interfaces (CODEC, JTAG, USB, UART), and the tool-chain flow (Editor → Compiler/Assembler → Linker → Loader → Debugger).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **professional chef in a commercial kitchen** versus a **home cook**.

- A **general-purpose microprocessor (GPP)** is like a home cook. The chef can do *everything* (boil, fry, bake, stew) but has to fetch every ingredient (data), every pot (memory location), and every tool (multiplier) from different shelves one at a time. This takes time when making a thousand identical dishes.

- A **DSP processor** is like a **professional commercial kitchen**: 
  - The **Harvard architecture** is the *separate ingredient racks* — one rack always has raw materials (data memory) and another always has recipes (program memory), so the chef can grab both simultaneously.
  - The **hardware MAC unit** is the *pre-assembled spice mix dispenser* — instead of measuring 5 spices one by one for every dish, the chef presses one button and gets the multiplication-and-accumulation in a single tick.
  - The **barrel shifter** is the *adjustable cutting blade* — it can slice ingredients (binary numbers) to any bit width in a single motion, no matter how large the batch.
  - The **zero-overhead looping** is the *conveyor belt* — the chef repeats the same step thousands of times without going back to read the recipe each time.

For a B.Tech student: *you don't need to cook* — you just need to recognize these "kitchen stations" on the DSP board and understand what each does in a single clock cycle.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in DSP Hardware

- **Instruction Cycle Time (T):** Determined by the CPU clock period.
$$T = \frac{1}{f_{\text{clk}}}$$
For the **TMS320C6713** at **225 MHz**, $T \approx 4.44$ **ns**.

- **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second):** Throughput metric.
$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$$
where **CPI** = Cycles Per Instruction.

- **MOPS / MFLOPS:** Million Operations / Million Floating-Point Operations Per Second.

- **Bit-Width:** Determines dynamic range and precision (16-bit fixed, 32-bit fixed, 32-bit float).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Value (KTU Exam Favorite):** The TMS320C67xx family operates at up to **300 MHz** and delivers up to **2400 MIPS / 1800 MFLOPS** — a benchmark examiners frequently cite.

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Typical DSP Processor

A DSP processor is built around five core operational pillars. The following is a structured breakdown of the *why* and *how* behind each pillar.

### Pillar 1: Harvard / Modified Harvard Memory Architecture
- **Why?** Signal processing needs to fetch an instruction **and** a data sample in the **same clock cycle**. A Von Neumann architecture (single bus) cannot do this — it would create a *Von Neumann bottleneck*.
- **How?** The processor has **physically separate program memory bus** and **data memory bus**. The *Modified Harvard* variant (used by TI C6000) further allows the data to be read from program memory when needed (for coefficient tables), providing flexibility.

### Pillar 2: Hardware Multiplier–Accumulator (MAC)
- **Why?** The core of every FIR filter, convolution, correlation, and FFT butterfly is the operation:
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$
Multiplying two 16-bit numbers and accumulating the result is the most common inner-loop operation.
- **How?** A dedicated **16 × 16 → 32-bit (or 32 × 32 → 64-bit) hardware multiplier** is wired directly to an **accumulator register** so the entire Multiply–Add operation completes in **a single instruction cycle**.

### Pillar 3: Barrel Shifter
- **Why?** Scaling (gain control), bit-alignment, and normalization (e.g., for floating-point mantissa) require shifting a binary word by an arbitrary number of bits **in one cycle**, not bit-by-bit over multiple cycles.
- **How?** A combinational-logic network of multiplexers performs any 0-to-15 (or 0-to-31) bit left/right shift in a single clock.

### Pillar 4: Specialized Addressing Modes
- **Why?** FIR/IIR filters need to step through circular buffers, bit-reversed buffers (for FFT), and circular coefficient tables.
- **How?** Address Generation Units (AGUs) support:
  - **Circular addressing** (modulo arithmetic on pointer)
  - **Bit-reversed addressing** (used in FFT)
  - **Post-increment / pre-decrement** (auto-update pointers)

### Pillar 5: Zero-Overhead Looping & Pipelined Execution
- **Why?** In a GPP, every `for` loop iteration consumes 2–4 instructions just to test, branch, and update the counter.
- **How?** DSPs have dedicated **loop counter registers** and a **loop instruction buffer**, so the loop body executes repeatedly *without* any branch overhead. Combined with deep instruction pipelines (C6000 uses an 8-stage pipeline), one new instruction finishes every cycle.

## 2.2 Internal Block Diagram of a Generic DSP Chip

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Program Fetch Unit | Reads instructions from program memory; manages pipeline |
| Instruction Dispatch / Decode | Decodes opcode, routes operands to functional units |
| Register File (A0–A15, B0–B15) | Two clustered register files for parallel issuing |
| L1 / L2 Memory | On-chip SRAM and cache (very low latency) |
| Data Cross paths (1X, 2X) | Allow data sharing between register files |
| Multiplier (M) | 16×16 or 32×32 hardware multiply |
| ALU (.L, .S) | Arithmetic, logical, shift, branch operations |
| Load/Store (.D) | Data memory access via AGU |
| EMIF | External Memory Interface (SDRAM, Flash, SRAM) |
| McBSP / McASP | Multichannel Buffered Serial Port for CODEC |
| Timer / GPIO / PWM | General control peripherals |
| DMA Controller | Moves data between peripherals/memory without CPU |
| JTAG | Test + real-time debug interface |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clock period | $T = 1 / f_{\text{clk}}$ | seconds |
| 2 | MIPS rating | $\text{MIPS} = f_{\text{clk}} / \text{CPI}$ | Millions of Instructions/s |
| 3 | MFLOPS rating | $\text{MFLOPS} = f_{\text{clk}} \times \text{ops/cycle} / 10^{6}$ | For floating-point DSPs |
| 4 | MAC throughput | One MAC per cycle | 1 sample processed every $T$ seconds |
| 5 | FIR filter output | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$ | Requires N MACs per sample |
| 6 | Sampling theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Nyquist criterion |
| 7 | Q15 fixed-point range | $-1 \leq x < 1 - 2^{-15}$ | Sign-magnitude 16-bit |
| 8 | Q15 conversion from float | $x_Q = \text{round}(x \cdot 2^{15})$ | Integer stored in 16 bits |
| 9 | Dynamic range of 16-bit | $\text{DR} = 6.02 \times N + 1.76$ dB | $N = 16 \Rightarrow 98$ dB |
| 10 | Real-time constraint | Total MACs per sample $\leq f_{\text{clk}} / f_s$ | For real-time execution |
| 11 | Power spectral density (periodogram) | $\hat{P}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^{2}$ | Uses FFT |
| 12 | Pipeline fill latency | $L_{\text{pipeline}}$ cycles before first result | E.g., C6713 has 8 stages |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Convention:** The vertical bar `$\vert$` is used in absolute-value expressions to keep Markdown table syntax intact. **Do not** write `|x|` inside a table cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio processing:** MP3/AAC decoders, equalizers, noise-cancelling headphones.
- **Telecommunications:** Modems (V.92), 4G/5G base-station channel cards, software-defined radio (SDR).
- **Medical imaging:** Ultrasound beamforming, MRI reconstruction (FFT-heavy).
- **Industrial control:** Motor control (FOC, PID at high PWM rates), vibration analysis.
- **Automotive ADAS:** Radar/sonar signal processing, sensor fusion.
- **Defence & Aerospace:** Electronic warfare, encrypted voice (vocoders), radar pulse-Doppler.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Real-Time Throughput Constraint for an FIR Filter

> **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):** A 64-tap FIR filter must run on a TMS320C6713 DSP clocked at **225 MHz**. Each filter tap requires **1 MAC instruction cycle**. The audio input sampling rate is **48 kHz**. Determine whether the system meets the real-time constraint.

### Step 1 — Identify the computational load per output sample
An N-tap FIR filter computes one output sample $y[n]$ using $N$ multiplications and $N-1$ additions. Since a single MAC instruction performs one multiplication *and* one accumulation:

$$\text{MACs per output sample} = N = 64$$

### Step 2 — Identify the time budget per output sample
Sampling period $T_s$:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{48 \times 10^{3}} = 20.833 \; \mu s$$

### Step 3 — Compute cycles available per sample
Each cycle is $T_{\text{clk}} = 1 / f_{\text{clk}}$:

$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{225 \times 10^{6}} = 4.444 \; ns$$

Cycles available in one sample period:

$$\text{Cycles}_{\text{avail}} = \frac{T_s}{T_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{20.833 \; \mu s}{4.444 \; ns} = \frac{20.833 \times 10^{-6}}{4.444 \times 10^{-9}}$$

### Step 4 — Perform the division explicitly
$$\text{Cycles}_{\text{avail}} = \frac{20.833}{4.444} \times 10^{3} \approx 4.688 \times 10^{3} = 4688 \; \text{cycles}$$

### Step 5 — Compare required vs. available
$$\text{Cycles}_{\text{req}} = N = 64$$
$$\text{Cycles}_{\text{avail}} = 4688$$

Since $64 \ll 4688$, the system easily meets real-time. The CPU utilization is:

$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{\text{Cycles}_{\text{req}}}{\text{Cycles}_{\text{avail}}} \times 100\% = \frac{64}{4688} \times 100\% \approx 1.365\%$$

> **Conclusion:** The DSP is over-spec'd for this single-channel task; it has $\approx 4688 / 64 \approx 73\times$ headroom, useful for multi-channel or higher-order filtering.

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Range of an N-bit Fixed-Point ADC/DSP Word

The signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) of an ideal N-bit uniform quantizer is a textbook result, derivable below.

### Step 1 — Define quantization error
For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$, the error $e = x_q - x$ lies in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$. Assuming uniform distribution:

$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad e \in \left[ -\frac{\Delta}{2}, \frac{\Delta}{2} \right]$$

### Step 2 — Compute mean-square error (variance)
$$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, p(e) \, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the integral
$$\int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de = \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} = \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^3}{24} - \left( -\frac{\Delta^3}{24} \right) = \frac{\Delta^3}{12}$$

### Step 4 — Combine
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

### Step 5 — Express signal power
For a full-scale sinusoidal input with peak-to-peak $\Delta \cdot 2^N$, the signal RMS is:

$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{(\Delta \cdot 2^{N-1})^2}{2} = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-2}}{2} = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-1}}{2} = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N-2}}{2}$$

For sine wave (form factor $1/\sqrt{2}$):
$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N}}{8}$$

### Step 6 — Compute SQNR
$$\text{SQNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\sigma_x^2}{\sigma_e^2} \right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 2^{2N} / 8}{\Delta^2 / 12} \right)$$

### Step 7 — Simplify
$$\text{SQNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{2^{2N} \cdot 12}{8} \right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N} \right)$$

$$\text{SQNR} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 N \log_{10}(2) \approx 1.76 + 6.02 N \; \text{dB}$$

### Step 8 — Plug in $N = 16$ (16-bit DSP word)
$$\text{SQNR}_{16} \approx 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \; \text{dB}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** A 16-bit fixed-point DSP word provides approximately **98 dB of dynamic range** — close to the threshold of human hearing. Going to 24-bit extends this to **146 dB** (studio audio), and 32-bit float extends to **~1500 dB** (scientific).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of DSP Family vs. Application

| Application | Bit-Width | Typical DSP | Key Feature Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low-power audio (hearing aid) | 16-bit fixed | TMS320C5505, ADSP-21xx | Ultra-low power, MAC, low voltage |
| High-fidelity audio (mixer) | 24/32-bit fixed | SHARC ADSP-21489 | Large on-chip SRAM, SIMD |
| Professional audio (effects) | 32-bit float | TMS320C67xx, SHARC 214xx | Hardware IEEE-754 float |
| Motor control (PWM, FOC) | 32-bit fixed/float | TMS320F28335 (C2000) | Control Law Accelerator, ePWM |
| Baseband telecom (LTE) | 16-bit fixed (vector) | TMS320C66x (KeyStone) | 8-core VLIW, hardware FFT |
| Image processing | 16/32-bit | DaVinci (DM64x/DM81x) | Video ports, hardware JPEG |
| General-purpose DSP class lab | 32-bit float | TMS320C6713 | Cache, EMIF, McBSP, CCS IDE |

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of a VLIW DSP (TMS320C6000 Series)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PMem["Program Memory Bus"]
    DMem["Data Memory Bus"]
    RegA["Register File A: A0 to A15"]
    RegB["Register File B: B0 to B15"]
    Fetch["Program Fetch Unit"]
    Decode["Instruction Dispatch and Decode"]
    M1["Multiplier M1: 16x16 or 32x32"]
    M2["Multiplier M2: 16x16 or 32x32"]
    L1["ALU L1: arithmetic and compare"]
    L2["ALU L2: arithmetic and compare"]
    S1["ALU S1: shift, branch, logical"]
    S2["ALU S2: shift, branch, logical"]
    D1["Load Store D1 with AGU"]
    D2["Load Store D2 with AGU"]
    EMIF["External Memory Interface EMIF"]
    McBSP["Multichannel Buffered Serial Port McBSP"]
    Timer["Timer 0, Timer 1, Timer 2"]
    DMA["Direct Memory Access Controller DMA"]
    JTAG["JTAG Test and Debug Port"]
    GPIO["General Purpose IO GPIO"]

    PMem --> Fetch
    Fetch --> Decode
    Decode --> RegA
    Decode --> RegB
    RegA --> M1
    RegA --> M2
    RegA --> L1
    RegA --> S1
    RegA --> D1
    RegB --> L2
    RegB --> S2
    RegB --> D2
    DMem --> D1
    DMem --> D2
    D1 --> DMem
    D2 --> DMem
    D1 --> EMIF
    D2 --> EMIF
    M1 --> RegA
    M2 --> RegB
    L1 --> RegA
    L2 --> RegB
    S1 --> RegA
    S2 --> RegB
    EMIF --> McBSP
    EMIF --> Timer
    EMIF --> DMA
    EMIF --> JTAG
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**List any four distinguishing architectural features of a DSP processor over a general-purpose microprocessor.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Harvard (or Modified Harvard) memory architecture** — separate program and data buses for simultaneous instruction fetch and data access.
2. **Hardware Multiplier–Accumulator (MAC) unit** — performs one multiplication and one accumulation in a single instruction cycle, essential for FIR/convolution.
3. **Multiple on-chip memories** — separate Program Cache, Data Cache, and L2 SRAM for low-latency access.
4. **Specialized addressing modes** — circular (modulo) addressing, bit-reversed addressing for FFT, and post-increment/decrement auto-update of pointers.
5. *Any other correct feature:* Zero-overhead looping, barrel shifter, single-cycle multiply.

*(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**What is meant by "zero-overhead looping" in a DSP? Why is it important for real-time filtering?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** Zero-overhead looping is a hardware feature in DSPs where dedicated loop-counter registers and an instruction buffer allow a loop body to be executed repeatedly *without* consuming any extra cycles for testing the counter, branching back, or updating the index.
- **Importance:** In real-time filtering (e.g., a 512-tap FIR running at 48 kHz), the loop runs millions of times per second. A GPP would waste 20–30% of cycles on loop overhead, but a DSP spends *zero* cycles, leaving the full CPU bandwidth for the actual MAC operations. This guarantees deterministic, jitter-free output timing.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Module Experiment 2 – Familiarization of DSP Hardware**

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of the TMS320C67xx family of DSP processors. Highlight the role of the two register files, the cross paths, and the eight functional units.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A 128-tap FIR filter must run on a TMS320C6713 DSP clocked at **225 MHz**. Audio is sampled at **44.1 kHz**. Each filter tap uses one MAC instruction. Determine the cycle budget per sample, the number of cycles available, and the CPU utilization. State whether the real-time constraint is satisfied. If we wish to process 4 channels simultaneously, what is the new utilization?

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare Fixed-Point and Floating-Point DSP processors. Give two example processors for each type. Explain Q15 fixed-point representation and convert the value $x = 0.625$ to Q15.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a block diagram, describe the typical signal flow of an audio application on a DSP development kit (microphone → CODEC → DSP → CODEC → speaker), naming the role of McBSP, DMA, EMIF, and JTAG.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — TMS320C67xx Internal Architecture (7 marks)**

The TMS320C67xx belongs to TI's C6000 VLIW family. Key blocks (refer to **Section 4.1** diagram):

- **Program Memory Bus & Data Memory Buses** (Modified Harvard): The C67xx has one program fetch bus (256 bits wide) and two data buses (one per data path), enabling an 8-instruction *packet* to be fetched every cycle.
- **Two register files (A and B):** Each contains 16 × 32-bit registers (A0–A15, B0–B15). The compiler partitions code so that one half of the functional units reads only from A and the other half from B, enabling parallel execution.
- **Cross paths (1X and 2X):** These two data paths allow one register from file A to feed a functional unit in the B-cluster and vice-versa, giving the compiler flexibility to balance the pipeline.
- **Eight functional units (.M1, .M2, .L1, .L2, .S1, .S2, .D1, .D2):**
  - **.M units** — 16×16-bit (or 32×32-bit) integer and floating-point multipliers. Two of them.
  - **.L units** — Integer/float ALU plus compare operations. Two of them.
  - **.S units** — Shifts, branches, byte-level logical, plus float add/sub on C67xx. Two of them.
  - **.D units** — Load/store with address-generation (AGU) for data memory access. Two of them.
- **On-chip memory:** L1P (Program cache, 4 KB), L1D (Data cache, 4 KB), L2 unified (up to 256 KB).
- **Peripherals:** EMIF (for SDRAM/Flash), McBSP, timers, GPIO, HPI (host port), DMA, JTAG.

> **Key takeaway (1 mark):** All 8 units can operate in parallel — this is the heart of VLIW parallelism. The CPU can issue up to 8 RISC-like instructions *per cycle* under compiler control.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

Given: $N = 128$ taps, $f_{\text{clk}} = 225$ MHz, $f_s = 44.1$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Cycles required per output sample:**
$$\text{Cycles}_{\text{req}} = N = 128 \text{ cycles/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Sampling period:**
$$T_s = \frac{1}{44.1 \times 10^3} = 22.676 \; \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Cycles available per sample:**
$$T_{\text{clk}} = \frac{1}{225 \times 10^6} = 4.444 \; ns$$

$$\text{Cycles}_{\text{avail}} = \frac{T_s}{T_{\text{clk}}} = \frac{22.676 \times 10^{-6}}{4.444 \times 10^{-9}} = 5102 \; \text{cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Single-channel utilization:**
$$U_1 = \frac{128}{5102} \times 100\% = 2.51\%$$

**Step 5 — Four-channel utilization:**
$$U_4 = 4 \times U_1 = 4 \times 2.51\% = 10.04\%$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
Since $U_4 = 10.04\% \ll 100\%$, the DSP comfortably processes 4 channels in real-time. The headroom ($\approx 90\%$) is available for additional processing (e.g., IIR filtering, FFT, or echo-cancellation).

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Fixed vs. Floating Point DSP (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Fixed-Point DSP | Floating-Point DSP |
|---|---|---|
| Data format | Integer (2's complement), Q-format | IEEE-754 single/double precision |
| Hardware complexity | Lower → cheaper, lower power | Higher → expensive, more power |
| Speed | Faster (no exponent handling) | Slightly slower per MAC |
| Dynamic range | Limited (≈ 6.02N dB) | Very large (≈ 1500 dB for 32-bit float) |
| Precision | Fixed, programmer manages scaling | Almost no scaling needed |
| Cost / power | Low | High |
| Example 1 | TMS320C5000 (16-bit fixed) | TMS320C67xx (32-bit float) |
| Example 2 | ADSP-2181 (16-bit fixed) | ADSP-21489 SHARC (32/40-bit float) |

**Q15 Representation:**
- In Q15, a 16-bit signed integer $X$ represents the fractional value $x = X / 2^{15}$, so the range is $[-1, \; 1 - 2^{-15}]$.
- **Conversion of $x = 0.625$:**
$$X = \text{round}(x \cdot 2^{15}) = \text{round}(0.625 \times 32768) = \text{round}(20480) = 20480$$
- In binary (16-bit): $0.625 = 0.101_2$, so $X = 0.101\,0000\,0000\,0000_2$ (right-padded zeros) = $0101\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 = 0x5000$.

**Part (b) — Audio Signal Flow (7 marks)**

(Refer to **Section 4.3** diagram.)

1. **Microphone** captures analog audio → fed to **CODEC ADC** (e.g., TLV320AIC23) which samples at 48 kHz with 16/24-bit resolution.
2. The **CODEC** serializes samples and transmits them to the DSP via the **McBSP** (Multichannel Buffered Serial Port) — a synchronous serial link with frame-sync and bit-clock lines.
3. The **DMA controller** can move incoming samples from the McBSP receive register directly into a circular buffer in **on-chip RAM** without CPU intervention, freeing the CPU for processing.
4. The **EMIF** (External Memory Interface) connects the DSP to **external SDRAM** (for large FFT buffers) and **external Flash** (for boot code).
5. The CPU core runs the **DSP algorithm** (FIR/IIR/FFT) using the **MAC unit, ALUs, and barrel shifter**; processed samples are placed in an output buffer.
6. Output samples flow back through **McBSP** to the **CODEC DAC** → analog audio → **Speaker**.
7. The **JTAG port** connects to the host PC's emulator (XDS510/XDS560/Blackhawk) for real-time debug and code download via Code Composer Studio.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Don't write "DSP is a microprocessor"** — examiners want the distinguishing features. Always mention *Harvard architecture, MAC, addressing modes*.
> - **Don't confuse MIPS with MHz** — MIPS divides by CPI; without CPI given, do not compute MIPS directly.
> - **Don't forget units** in numericals — if the answer is cycles, write "cycles"; if it is utilization, write "%".
> - **Don't confuse McBSP with I2S** — they are *similar* but not identical. I2S is a subset/compatible mode of McBSP in many TI chips.
> - **Don't write "Q15" as a value** — Q15 is a *format*, not a number. The number 20480 is the *integer representation* of 0.625 in Q15.
> - **Don't draw the CPU block without labeling data paths** — fetch/decode/execute stages must be visible.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DSP ≠ GPP.** The *only* reason DSPs exist is to do MAC operations fast and deterministically.
- **Harvard architecture** = separate code and data buses. **Modified Harvard** = code bus can also be used to read data (coefficient tables).
- **MAC = multiply + accumulate in 1 cycle** — the heart of FIR/convolution/FFT butterfly.
- **Barrel shifter** performs any-bit shift in 1 cycle (used for Q-format scaling and normalization).
- **Zero-overhead looping** = loops execute repeatedly without branch overhead → deterministic timing.
- **Circular addressing** = pointer wraps around a buffer automatically; perfect for sliding-window FIR.
- **Bit-reversed addressing** = pointer step in bit-reversed order; perfect for in-place FFT.
- **Q15 format** uses 16 bits to represent numbers in $[-1, +1)$. Multiply a Q15 by Q15 to get Q30, then shift right by 15 to renormalize.
- **Dynamic range** of N-bit fixed-point: $\text{DR} \approx 6.02N + 1.76$ dB. For 16-bit: **≈ 98 dB**.
- **Real-time check:** $\text{cycles required per sample} \leq f_{\text{clk}} / f_s$.
- **TMS320C6713 specs to memorize:** 225 MHz, 32-bit float, 8 functional units, VLIW, 8-stage pipeline, 4 KB L1P + 4 KB L1D, 256 KB L2.
- **CCS = Code Composer Studio** is TI's IDE; **VisualDSP++** is Analog Devices' IDE. Both support C/C++ and assembly.
- **JTAG** is the standard hardware debug port — always mention it.
- **CODEC** is the bridge between analog world and DSP — interfaces via **McBSP** or **I2S** in serial.
- **DMA** moves data autonomously — vital for freeing the CPU to do pure signal processing.
- **EMIF** expands memory beyond the chip — connects to SDRAM/Flash.
- **EMI/EMC, power decoupling, and clock jitter** matter in board-level design — but KTU questions usually focus on the *chip-level* architecture.

topics.title: Linear Convolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECL507 - PBECL507
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Linear Convolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Linear Convolution

**Linear Convolution** is a fundamental mathematical operation in Digital Signal Processing (DSP) that describes the output of a **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** discrete-time system when a discrete-time input signal is passed through it, characterized by the system's impulse response.

For two discrete-time sequences $x[n]$ and $h[n]$, the linear convolution operation is mathematically defined as:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$$

Where:
- $x[n]$ is the **input signal sequence** of length $L$
- $h[n]$ is the **impulse response** of the LTI system of length $M$
- $y[n]$ is the **output sequence** of length $N = L + M - 1$
- $*$ denotes the **convolution operator**
- $k$ is the dummy summation index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The output length of linear convolution is always $N = L + M - 1$, where $L$ and $M$ are lengths of the two input sequences. This is a frequently tested 2-mark concept.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**The Echo Analogy** 🎙️

Imagine you are standing in a large empty hall and you clap your hands once. Instead of hearing a single sharp clap, you hear a series of fading echoes. Why? Because the sound waves bounce off the walls and reach your ear at different times with different intensities.

In this analogy:
- Your **single clap** is the **input signal** $x[n]$
- The **hall's acoustic response** (how it modifies the sound) is the **impulse response** $h[n]$
- The **actual sound you hear** (original clap + decaying echoes) is the **output** $y[n]$

Linear convolution is essentially the mathematical way of saying: *"Every time the input signal does something, the system's response to that 'something' gets added to the total output, with appropriate time delays."*

### A Simpler Analogy: The Sliding Multiplication

Think of convolution as a **sliding, flipping, multiplying, and adding** operation:
1. **Flip** one signal (say $h[n]$) about the origin to get $h[-k]$
2. **Slide** this flipped version across the other signal
3. At every slide position $n$, **multiply overlapping samples** point by point
4. **Sum up** all the multiplied values → this gives $y[n]$

## 1.3 Key Conceptual Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Equivalent Interpretations of Linear Convolution:**
> 1. **Mathematical View:** Discrete summation operation between two sequences.
> 2. **System View:** The response of an LTI system to an arbitrary input.
> 3. **Signal View:** A "blending" or "smoothing" operation where one signal shapes the other.

The convolution sum essentially models the principle of **superposition** — each shifted, scaled copy of $h[n]$ (weighted by the samples of $x[n]$) contributes additively to form $y[n]$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation

The linear convolution sum is derived from the **discrete-time decomposition property**. Any discrete-time signal $x[n]$ can be expressed as a weighted sum of shifted unit impulses $\delta[n-k]$:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k]$$

When passed through an LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$, each impulse component produces a scaled, shifted output $x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$. By the **superposition principle**, the total output is the sum of all such contributions:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**The Convolution Procedure (for finite sequences of lengths $L$ and $M$):**

1. **Step 1 — Indexing:** Write $x[k]$ indexed by $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, L-1$.
2. **Step 2 — Time Reversal:** Form $h[-k]$ by flipping $h[k]$ about $k = 0$.
3. **Step 3 — Time Shifting:** Shift the flipped sequence by $n$ samples to get $h[n-k]$.
4. **Step 4 — Sample-wise Multiplication:** Multiply $x[k]$ and $h[n-k]$ element by element.
5. **Step 5 — Summation:** Add all products for that shift position $n$.
6. **Step 6 — Iteration:** Repeat Steps 3 to 5 for all $n \in [0, L+M-2]$.
7. **Step 7 — Output Formation:** Collect all summed values to form $y[n]$ of length $N = L + M - 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:** The mnemonic **"F-S-M-A-S"** (Flip, Shift, Multiply, Add, Slide) is widely used in KTU board valuations for describing the convolution algorithm.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Property / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Engineering Significance** |
|------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Convolution Sum (General) | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$ | Defines output of LTI system |
| Output Length | $N = L + M - 1$ | Determines buffer/memory size in DSP processors |
| Convolution with Impulse | $x[n] * \delta[n-n_0] = x[n-n_0]$ | Acts as a delay/shift operator |
| Convolution with Unit Step | $x[n] * u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ | Produces a running sum (accumulator) |
| Commutative Property | $x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n]$ | Order of sequences is interchangeable |
| Associative Property | $(x[n] * h_1[n]) * h_2[n] = x[n] * (h_1[n] * h_2[n])$ | Cascade of LTI systems can be rearranged |
| Distributive Property | $x[n] * (h_1[n] + h_2[n]) = x[n] * h_1[n] + x[n] * h_2[n]$ | Parallel systems add their impulse responses |
| Convolution with Impulse Train | $x[n] * \sum_{k} \delta[n-kN] = $ periodic repetition | Basis of sampling theorem and DFT |
| Convolution in Frequency Domain | $x[n] * h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega}) \cdot H(e^{j\omega})$ | Multiplication in frequency ↔ Convolution in time |
| Convolution Theorem (DFT) | $\text{DFT}(x[n] * h[n]) = X[k] \cdot H[k]$ (with zero-padding) | Foundation of fast convolution algorithms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse with Circular Convolution:** When sequences are zero-padded to length $N \geq L + M - 1$, **linear convolution equals circular convolution** computed via DFT. This is the cornerstone of the **Overlap-Add** and **Overlap-Save** fast convolution methods.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Linear convolution is the backbone of numerous engineering applications:

- **Digital Filtering:** FIR filters compute $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$, which is precisely a linear convolution.
- **Audio Processing:** Reverberation, echo generation, and equalization in music production.
- **Image Processing:** 2-D convolution is used in edge detection (Sobel, Prewitt), blurring, and sharpening kernels.
- **Communication Systems:** Channel equalization where the transmitted signal is convolved with the channel impulse response.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG and EEG signal filtering to remove noise (e.g., 50 Hz powerline interference).
- **Radar and Sonar:** Matched filtering correlates transmitted and received pulses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Convolution Sum from LTI System Properties

**Starting Point:** An LTI system is completely characterized by its impulse response $h[n]$, defined as the system's response to a unit impulse $\delta[n]$:

$$h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$$

**Step 1:** Any arbitrary input signal $x[n]$ can be expressed as a sum of weighted, shifted impulses using the **sifting property** of the unit impulse:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k]$$

**Step 2:** Apply the LTI system operator $T\{\cdot\}$ to both sides:

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = T\left\{\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot \delta[n-k]\right\}$$

**Step 3:** By the **linearity** property, $T\{\cdot\}$ can be brought inside the summation:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot T\{\delta[n-k]\}$$

**Step 4:** By the **time-invariance** property, a delayed input $\delta[n-k]$ produces a delayed output $h[n-k]$:

$$T\{\delta[n-k]\} = h[n-k]$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back yields the convolution sum:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$$

$$\therefore y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem:** Compute the linear convolution $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ for:
- $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\}$ with $L = 3$, indexed from $n = 0$
- $h[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ with $M = 4$, indexed from $n = 0$

**Expected Output Length:** $N = L + M - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6$, so $n$ ranges from $0$ to $5$.

### Method 1: Direct Sum (Tabular/Array Method)

**Step 1:** Write $x[k]$ as a column and $h[-k]$ (flipped) as a row.

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{\text{flipped}} &= h[-k] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\} \text{ for } k = 3, 2, 1, 0 \\
\text{Equivalent indexed form: } & h[n-k] \text{ shifts with } n
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $y[n]$ for each $n$:

**For $n = 0$:**

$$y[0] = \sum_{k=0}^{0} x[k] \cdot h[0-k] = x[0] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$$

**For $n = 1$:**

$$y[1] = \sum_{k=0}^{1} x[k] \cdot h[1-k] = x[0] \cdot h[1] + x[1] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3$$

**For $n = 2$:**

$$y[2] = \sum_{k=0}^{2} x[k] \cdot h[2-k] = x[0]h[2] + x[1]h[1] + x[2]h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 6$$

**For $n = 3$:**

$$y[3] = \sum_{k=1}^{2} x[k] \cdot h[3-k] = x[1]h[2] + x[2]h[1] = 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 5$$

(Note: $x[0]h[3] = 0$ since $h[3]$ is the last valid index; equivalently, $x[3] = 0$ since $x$ has only indices 0,1,2.)

**For $n = 4$:**

$$y[4] = \sum_{k=2}^{2} x[k] \cdot h[4-k] = x[2] \cdot h[2] = 3 \cdot 1 = 3$$

**For $n = 5$:**

$$y[5] = \sum_{k=3}^{2} x[k] \cdot h[5-k] = 0$$

(Since the summation range is empty — both $x[3] = 0$ and $h[3] = 0$ effectively.)

**Final Result:**

$$y[n] = \{1, 3, 6, 5, 3, 0\} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$$

### Method 2: Tabular (Matrix) Method — Concise Form

This is the **fastest method** recommended for KTU exams. Multiply each sample of $x[n]$ by the entire $h[n]$ and place at the appropriate shifted position:

| $x[n]$ | $h[n]$ Coefficients | Placed At Index |
|:------:|:-------------------:|:---------------:|
| $x[0] = 1$ | $\mathbf{1, 1, 1, 1}$ | $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ |
| $x[1] = 2$ | $2, \mathbf{2, 2, 2}$ | $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$ |
| $x[2] = 3$ | $3, 3, \mathbf{3, 3}$ | $n = 2, 3, 4, 5$ |

**Sum column-by-column to obtain $y[n]$:**

$$y[n] = \{1,\ (1+2),\ (1+2+3),\ (1+2+3),\ (2+3),\ 3\} = \{1, 3, 6, 5, 3, 0\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In the matrix/tabular method, the bold values represent the diagonal that aligns to produce the maximum output sample. For sequences that resemble rectangular or triangular pulses, the convolution result takes the shape of a **triangular pulse** — a pattern frequently tested.

## 3.3 Verification via Length Formula

$$N_y = N_x + N_h - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6 \checkmark$$

The computed output has 6 samples, matching the theoretical length. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — Linear Convolution Process

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the step-by-step execution of the linear convolution algorithm, which is the core concept tested in KTU Module Experiment 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Input Sequences x n and h n] --> B[Identify Lengths: L and M]
    B --> C[Compute Output Length: N = L + M - 1]
    C --> D[Initialize n = 0]
    D --> E[Flip h k to form h -k]
    E --> F[Shift flipped sequence by n to get h n-k]
    F --> G[Multiply x k and h n-k point by point]
    G --> H[Sum all products to obtain y n]
    H --> I[Store y n in output buffer]
    I --> J{Is n less than N minus 1?}
    J -->|YES| K[Increment n by 1]
    K --> F
    J -->|NO| L[Return Output Sequence y n]
    L --> M[END]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — LTI System View

This block diagram shows how linear convolution is implemented in a real DSP hardware/software context, mapping the inputs, the LTI system, and the convolution output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x n] -->|Discrete Samples| B[Input Buffer of Length L]
    B --> C[LTI System with Impulse Response h n]
    C --> D[Multiplier Array: x k times h n-k]
    D --> E[Accumulator: Sum of Products]
    E --> F[Output Sequence y n of Length N]
    F --> G[Output Buffer of Length L plus M minus 1]
    G --> H[Processed Output Stream]
```

## 4.3 Operation Sequence Diagram — Flip, Shift, Multiply, Add

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sequence x n] -->|Origin| A1[x k indexed by k]
    B[Sequence h n] -->|Time Reversal| B1[h -k flipped]
    B1 -->|Shift by n samples| B2[h n-k]
    A1 -->|Sample-wise Multiply| C[Product Array: x k times h n-k]
    B2 -->|Sample-wise Multiply| C
    C -->|Summation over k| D[Output Sample y n at Shift n]
    D -->|Slide n from 0 to L plus M minus 2| E[Complete Output y n]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU practicals:** In the linear convolution experiment (Module Experiment 2), students typically verify these operations graphically using stem plots in MATLAB/Python. The "flip-shift-multiply-add" cycle is what you must demonstrate in the lab record.
